title: Religion in NGE Split
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 03, 2007 8:30 pm *Sigh* NGE is NOT religious, but it used religous texts to inspire its story. That almost sounds contradictory itself, no? One can easily apply spiritual/religous 'meanings' to NGE because it takes so many of its concepts from those sources. But what I've said from the beginning that these religious sources of inspiration can be taken on another level. While NGE might not be a religious series, it is DEFINITELY a psychological/philosophical series. And what it does is take religious concepts, and puts a more worldly, philosophical spin on it. The idea of the 'fruits', the original sin, etc. can all be taken in more contexts than their original religious one. I keep repeating this, but I just hate when people say that religion has no place in NGE. It's basically saying "that which inspired NGE has no place in NGE". As much as NGE turned religious ideas into science fiction, it still maintains a strong spiritual element even if it's not entirely religious. We all know NGE works on a multitude of levels. And perhaps the reason it's such a great series overall is that all of these different levels work with one another while at the same time working great on their on. Yes, you can take NGE as a pure SF narrative. But it wasn't meant to be a pure SF narrative. To take that literally is to miss a large portion of what NGE is about. So while I would conceed that the SF narrative side of NGE has no religious meanings, there are definitely layers of those elements at work - even if they're just alluded to, and even if they hold no real 'meaning' to the narrative and only serve as context and a nod to their influences.

[]:             Quote Post by 20XD6_Tyrant » Tue Jul 03, 2007 8:32 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: That doesn't make any sense. Your posts seem contradictory. Either you think the Angels are messengers from God per the Bible or you accept the revelations of the CI, which I might add Anno had a part in making.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 03, 2007 8:36 pm 20XD6_Tyrant wrote: Didn't Anno say he was merely being pedantic when he tacked on the religious symbolism?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Tue Jul 03, 2007 8:36 pm 7 posts. Oh dear. "Mario! Our topic is in a different thread!"

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 03, 2007 9:03 pm I'm now officially requesting a split (if ya don't mind): Ornette wrote: And no, the religious symbolism did not inspire NGE, Tsurimaki himself even said they would have used something else if they knew beforehand how popular it would have become in the west.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 03, 2007 9:31 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Man wanting to become God: Relevent in both NGE and the Bible Man messing with what they shouldn't and being punished: Relevent in both NGE and the Bible Post punishment, Men trying to create with their new-found knowledge: Relevent in both NGE and the Bible

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 03, 2007 9:45 pm Yeah, and SF borrows a lot from religion (philosophy as well) to inspire a lot of its ideas. Religion fits VERY nicely into Science Fiction and Fantasy. Their stories have more in common with those genres than any other (raising the dead, parting oceans, meta-physical wars between good and evil, etc.) But I think how NGE applies these ideas is a little different. I'm not saying ALL of NGE was inspired by Religious texts. I think it's pretty much a mish-mash of common SF and anime ideas, mixed with philosophy, psychology, and religion to make up its whole. NGE has two very obvious layers to it: first, there's the SF narrative. This is the entire idea of the FAR and the Seeds coming to and populating Earth and all that stuff. Then, there's the psychology/philosophy side of NGE. I mean, that's REALLY why the series was created in the first place. Anno uses much of the narrative to tell this part all along as allegory and allusions (Everything from the Evas to Rei works on more than just its base, SF level). The religious element, to me, works on both levels simultaneously. They take religious ideas and incorporate them into the SF story. On THIS level, these ideas have no meaning other than in the SF narrative itself. However, on the psychological/philosophical level they do have a deeper meaning. It's obvious they use psychological terms (Hedgehog's Dilemma, for example) to illustrate the points Anno wanted to make regarding his depression. I think they very purposefully picked religious ideas that could be applied - not just to the narrative side - but to this philosophy/psychological side as well. And I think THAT'S why so many people familiar with religion and spirituality can get something out of NGE on that level. I just think they make the mistake of assuming all of NGE is based around these ideas. And I'm arguing that it's only one part of what makes up NGE's world. I think the only thing to argue over is how significant it is on any level. But I do wonder why so may here get up in arms when people bring up religious/spiritual 'meanings' in NGE. Would NGE be any less great of a work if it was ENTIRELY inspired by religion? To me, being an Agnostic I think religion and philosophy are so closely related (the differences are smaller than most realize) that I fully believe a series about one or the other can usually easily be applied to the other side.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Tue Jul 03, 2007 9:58 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: But I do wonder why so may here get up in arms when people bring up religious/spiritual 'meanings' in NGE

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Tue Jul 03, 2007 10:00 pm Well, there's nothing to say it isn't (perhaps unintentionally) borrowing stuff that appears in the bible (there are only 7 types of stories, and all that stuff). If I replace every mention of "God" in NGE with "Bob", the names of the Angels changed to "Mr. C" through "Mr. L", the FoK/FoL to "The Paradox Machine"/"The HangSeng Divider", all the crosses with infinity symbols, etc. I would never have thought there were any religious inspiration. Eva Yojimbo wrote: But I do wonder why so may here get up in arms when people bring up religious/spiritual 'meanings' in NGE. Would NGE be any less great of a work if it was ENTIRELY inspired by religion? To me, being an Agnostic I think religion and philosophy are so closely related (the differences are smaller than most realize) that I fully believe a series about one or the other can usually easily be applied to the other side.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jul 03, 2007 11:38 pm Anonymous_Evafan wrote: It's frustrating that people are soo oooo caught up in religion that they can't let go of it and try to force it into places it's not suposed to be.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Tue Jul 03, 2007 11:54 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: When you have a series mentioning things like the Tree of Life, Fruit of Life/Knowledge, Lance of Longinus, etc. it isn't exactly like they're REACHING to find Religion within the series. It's pretty much on display from the beginning.

[]:             Quote Post by BobBQ » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:08 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: "Why are Seele trying to reach the path to Bob?"

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:11 am Anonymous_Evafan wrote: to truely understand this story you must look past what you think you see and aknowledge what the story is telling you

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:21 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: If religious texts served as no inspiration they could've just pulled these names from anywhere.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:23 am Anonymous_Evafan wrote: The story could have worked with a made up religion just was well.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:35 am That's certainly not the way it comes off dude. Anyways what rubs me the wrong way is the crowd that insists the Angels are messengers of God, that the plot is a war with God, Adam and Lilith were married, and the Spear is the one that peirced Christ at which piont they try to use the Bible to prove it completely ignoring the fact that the story itself tells them that the Angels are another form of Human that Adam gave birth to. What started this whole round was someones refusal to accept that Adam did indeed give birth to the Angels and that the Lilim evolved from a primordial soup sent by an alien race in favor of "religious meaning". Such aguements seem to pop up every few months or so and we spend hours arguing with them about it. This is the issue here, not where these themes came from.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 04, 2007 12:41 am Anonymous_Evafan wrote: This is the issue here, not where these themes came from.

[]:             Quote Post by Anonymous_Evafan » Wed Jul 04, 2007 1:06 am That is my issue here, always was always will be.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Jul 04, 2007 1:14 am You don't own it! I'm taking it up now too. ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Jul 04, 2007 1:29 am @Eva Yojimbo That's C S Lewis. @Topic As art therapy, Anno's depression expressed itself is exploration of alienation, and the whole question of what it is to be human. Religion is one of the ways that humans have in the past made up answers to that question. NGE's use of Qaballistic names is exactly the sort of thing that a western production might have grabbed at Buddhist names -- vaguely religious and definitely exotic. Behind the superfices, the transcendentalism is fairly stock SF -- see here .

Article: Religion in NGE Split
 This is the entire idea of the FAR and the Seeds coming to and populating Earth and all that stuff. Then, there's the psychology/philosophy side of NGE. I mean, that's REALLY why the series was created in the first place. Anno uses much of the narrative to tell this part all along as allegory and allusions (Everything from the Evas to Rei works on more than just its base, SF level). The religious element, to me, works on both levels simultaneously. They take religious ideas and incorporate them into the SF story. On THIS level, these ideas have no meaning other than in the SF narrative itself. However, on the psychological/philosophical level they do have a deeper meaning. It's obvious they use psychological terms (Hedgehog's Dilemma, for example) to illustrate the points Anno wanted to make regarding his depression. I think they very purposefully picked religious ideas that could be applied - not just to the narrative side - but to this philosophy/psychological side as well. And I think THAT'S why so many people familiar with religion and spirituality can get something out of NGE on that level. I just think they make the mistake of assuming all of NGE is based around these ideas. And I'm arguing that it's only one part of what makes up NGE's world. I think the only thing to argue over is how significant it is on any level. But I do wonder why so may here get up in arms when people bring up religious/spiritual 'meanings' in NGE. Would NGE be any less great of a work if it was ENTIRELY inspired by religion? To me, being an Agnostic I think religion and philosophy are so closely related (the differences are smaller than most realize) that I fully believe a series about one or the other can usually easily be applied to the other side.
